title: VFD Output Reactor: When to Install on PowerFlex & GS10/GS20 Drives
meta_description: When to install a VFD output reactor on PowerFlex, GS10, or GS20 drives. Protect motors from voltage spikes, reduce failures, extend life.

h2_sections.heading: What Is a VFD Output Reactor?
h2_sections.body: A VFD output reactor is a line impedance device installed between the drive output and the motor. It’s a coil of copper around a steel core, designed to smooth current and suppress high-frequency voltage spikes caused by fast switching in IGBTs. On PowerFlex 525, 750, GS10, or GS20 drives, these spikes can exceed 1,000 V/µs rise time over long motor leads, damaging motor insulation. Output reactors add 3–5% impedance, limiting peak current and dI/dt stress. They don’t fix grounding issues or replace proper cable shielding, but they significantly reduce electrical noise and stress on motor windings. For runs over 50 feet, especially with older motors not rated for inverter-duty, an output reactor is not optional — it’s preventative maintenance. You’ll typically see them mounted in the same cabinet as the VFD or in the motor starter assembly. Use a reactor rated for the drive’s full load current and system voltage — 480V for most industrial PowerFlex installations.

h2_sections.heading: Common Causes of Motor Insulation Failure with VFDs
h2_sections.body: 1. Long motor leads — over 50 feet increases reflected wave voltage, spiking up to 2x DC bus voltage. 2. Non-inverter-rated motors — standard NEMA motors lack insulation to withstand high dV/dt stress. 3. High carrier frequency — GS10/GS20 drives above 8 kHz increase switching noise and heat in windings. 4. Multiple motors on one VFD — uneven impedance leads to voltage imbalance and overvoltage on one motor. 5. Poor grounding — ground loops or shared neutrals allow noise to feedback into control circuits. 6. Rapid acceleration/deceleration — high torque demands increase current harmonics and heat in the stator. 7. Ambient heat — motor temperatures above 40°C degrade insulation faster when combined with VFD stress. These factors combine silently — you won’t see damage until Phase A shorts to ground. By then, it’s a rewind or replacement. Install an output reactor before failure — not after. At a paper mill in Wisconsin, skipping reactors on five 75HP hydraulic power unit motors led to three failures in 14 months. After adding reactors, zero failures in 18 months.

h2_sections.heading: Step-by-Step: Installing an Output Reactor on a PowerFlex 525
h2_sections.body: 1. Power down the VFD and lock out the 480V supply. Verify no voltage at terminals T1/T2/T3 with a Fluke 87V. 2. Disconnect motor leads from VFD output. Label each lead. 3. Mount the reactor near the drive — keep output wiring short. Use 1/4" steel mounting hardware. 4. Connect VFD output (T1, T2, T3) to reactor input terminals. Use copper lugs rated for 75°C. Torque to 35 lb-in for #4 AWG, 50 lb-in for 1/0. 5. Run new leads from reactor output to motor. Use shielded cable if leads exceed 75 feet. Ground shield at drive end only. 6. In PowerFlex 525 Menu, go to Parameter P039 (Carrier Frequency). Reduce from 8 kHz to 4 kHz to limit switching noise. 7. Power up. Run motor at 30 Hz. Check reactor temperature — it should not exceed 80°C. Use an IR gun like the Fluke 62 Max. No alarms? Test up to full speed. If OL1 persists, check motor insulation with a megger. A 1000V megger should read over 5 MΩ. Below 2 MΩ? Motor is compromised — reactor won’t save it.

h2_sections.heading: When to Escalate Beyond an Output Reactor
h2_sections.body: An output reactor isn't a fix-all. If you’ve installed one and still see overvoltage faults (like F004 on PowerFlex 525) or motor insulation breakdown, escalate. First, rule out input power issues — use a power quality analyzer to check for line imbalance or transients. If voltage sags below 400V on a 480V system, the drive may fault before the reactor even engages. Second, if the motor fails within 6 months of reactor install, suspect pre-existing winding damage. Test with a megger and surge tester — a 20% imbalance between phases means rewind. Third, for drives feeding multiple motors, consider a dV/dt filter instead — reactors don’t balance load sharing. Fourth, if you’re running GS20 drives with long leads and encountering noise in Allen-Bradley PLC communications (e.g., 1769-L30ER faults), add common mode chokes and verify shield grounding. In one plant, VFD noise was crashing ControlLogix racks — reactors helped, but only fixing the ground bus stopped the faults. When in doubt, pull the motor leads and bench-test. If you don’t have the manual, snap a photo of the drive and fault code — Mira in FactoryLM pulls the exact parameter list in 10 seconds, no digging.

cta_text: Stop searching for manuals or waiting on OEM support. Diagnose faults and access your documentation instantly at app.factorylm.com.
